title: Ransomware Attack on Production Systems
scenario: At 3 AM, your monitoring alerts show encrypted files spreading across production servers. The attacker has left a ransom note demanding $2M in Bitcoin. Critical customer data may be affected.
objective: Contain the attack, assess damage, restore operations, and prevent recurrence.
keySteps: Immediately isolate affected systems from network
Activate incident response team and escalation chain
Preserve forensic evidence before any remediation
Identify patient zero and attack vector
Assess scope of encryption and data exfiltration
Notify legal, compliance, and executive leadership
Restore from clean backups (do not pay ransom)
Conduct post-incident forensics
Notify affected customers and regulators per breach notification laws
commonMistakes: Paying the ransom (no guarantee of decryption, funds criminal activity)
Rebooting systems before forensic capture
Failing to isolate systems quickly enough
Not preserving chain of custody for evidence
idealResponse: Isolate within minutes, preserve evidence, restore from offline backups, notify stakeholders, conduct full forensic investigation, implement lessons learned.
followUpQuestions: How do you determine if data was exfiltrated before encryption?
What backup strategy prevents ransomware from encrypting backups?
realWorldContext: Colonial Pipeline paid $4.4M ransom in 2021. Most security experts advise against paying — it funds criminal operations and doesn't guarantee recovery.

title: Insider Threat: Data Exfiltration by Departing Employee
scenario: HR notifies you that a senior engineer resigned yesterday. Your DLP system alerts that 50GB of data was copied to a personal USB drive and uploaded to a personal cloud storage account in the past week.
objective: Investigate the incident, preserve evidence, assess damage, and take appropriate action.
keySteps: Immediately revoke all access (SSO, VPN, cloud, code repos)
Preserve logs: DLP alerts, access logs, email, Slack
Identify what data was taken (classification, sensitivity)
Determine if data included trade secrets, customer PII, or IP
Engage legal counsel before contacting the employee
File police report if criminal activity suspected
Notify affected customers if PII was involved
Review and tighten offboarding procedures
commonMistakes: Confronting the employee before legal review
Failing to preserve logs before revoking access
Not checking if data was already shared externally
Delaying access revocation
idealResponse: Immediate access revocation, evidence preservation, legal engagement, thorough investigation, appropriate escalation based on data sensitivity.
followUpQuestions: How do you prevent insider threats proactively?
What DLP controls would have detected this earlier?
realWorldContext: Tesla sued a former employee in 2023 for stealing 26,000 files before joining a competitor. Proper offboarding and DLP controls are critical.

title: DDoS Attack Taking Down Customer-Facing API
scenario: Your API is receiving 10M requests/second from thousands of IPs globally. Response times have degraded to 30 seconds and the service is effectively down. Normal traffic is 50K requests/second.
objective: Mitigate the attack, restore service, and implement long-term protections.
keySteps: Enable AWS Shield Advanced or Cloudflare DDoS protection immediately
Activate WAF rate limiting rules
Enable CloudFront to absorb traffic at edge
Block attacking IP ranges at network level (if identifiable)
Scale up infrastructure if volumetric attack
Engage CDN/ISP for upstream filtering
Analyze traffic patterns to distinguish attack from legitimate traffic
Implement CAPTCHA for suspicious traffic patterns
Post-incident: implement rate limiting, bot detection, traffic baseline monitoring
commonMistakes: Trying to block individual IPs (attackers rotate IPs)
Scaling up without filtering (expensive and ineffective)
Not having DDoS protection pre-configured before attack
idealResponse: Pre-configured DDoS protection absorbs attack at edge, WAF rules filter malicious patterns, service restored within minutes.
followUpQuestions: What is the difference between volumetric, protocol, and application layer DDoS?
How do you distinguish DDoS traffic from a legitimate traffic spike?
realWorldContext: GitHub experienced a 1.35 Tbps DDoS attack in 2018 — mitigated in 10 minutes using Akamai's scrubbing centers.

title: Kubernetes Pods Crash-Looping in Production
scenario: Multiple pods in your payment service are in CrashLoopBackOff state. The deployment was updated 20 minutes ago. Customer payments are failing.
objective: Diagnose and resolve the crash loop, restore service, and prevent recurrence.
keySteps: kubectl get pods -n payment — identify affected pods
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> — check events and exit codes
kubectl logs <pod-name> --previous — check logs from crashed container
Check if issue started with recent deployment (kubectl rollout history)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/payment-service — rollback if deployment caused it
Check resource limits — OOMKilled exit code 137 means memory limit exceeded
Check liveness probe configuration — too aggressive probes cause restarts
Check ConfigMap/Secret changes — missing env vars cause startup failures
commonMistakes: Not checking previous logs (current logs may be empty if crash is immediate)
Not rolling back immediately when deployment is the cause
Ignoring exit codes (137=OOM, 1=app error, 139=segfault)
idealResponse: Identify root cause from logs/events within 5 minutes, rollback if deployment-related, fix root cause, redeploy with fix.
followUpQuestions: How do you implement readiness probes to prevent traffic to unhealthy pods?
How do you set appropriate memory limits to prevent OOMKill?
realWorldContext: CrashLoopBackOff is one of the most common Kubernetes production issues — usually caused by missing config, OOM, or failed health checks.

title: Design a Payment Processing System
scenario: Design a payment processing system for an e-commerce platform handling 10,000 transactions per second at peak, with 99.99% availability, PCI DSS compliance, and support for multiple payment methods (cards, wallets, bank transfers).
objective: Design a scalable, secure, compliant payment architecture.
keySteps: Define components: payment gateway, processor integration, fraud detection, ledger, reconciliation
Idempotency: unique transaction IDs prevent double charges
Saga pattern for distributed transaction across payment, inventory, order services
PCI DSS scope reduction: tokenization (never store raw card data), use Stripe/Braintree as payment processor
Fraud detection: real-time ML scoring before authorization
Ledger: append-only, double-entry bookkeeping, immutable audit trail
Reconciliation: daily batch reconciliation with payment processors
Availability: multi-region active-active, circuit breakers for processor failures
commonMistakes: Storing raw card numbers (PCI DSS violation)
Synchronous calls to all services (latency and availability risk)
No idempotency (double charges on retry)
Single payment processor (no fallback)
idealResponse: Tokenized card storage, async saga for transaction flow, real-time fraud detection, multi-processor fallback, immutable ledger, automated reconciliation.
followUpQuestions: How do you handle partial failures in a payment saga?
How do you implement 3D Secure authentication?
realWorldContext: Stripe processes hundreds of billions in payments annually using a similar architecture with tokenization and idempotency at its core.

title: Design a Secure Multi-Agent Customer Support System
scenario: Design an agentic AI system for customer support that can handle billing inquiries, technical troubleshooting, and account management. The system must be secure, auditable, and prevent unauthorized actions.
objective: Design a production-grade agentic system with security guardrails.
keySteps: Orchestrator agent routes to specialized sub-agents (billing, technical, account)
Tool definitions with least-privilege: billing agent can READ but not MODIFY billing data
Human-in-the-loop for high-risk actions (refunds >$100, account deletion)
Input guardrails: PII detection, prompt injection scanner on all user inputs
Output guardrails: response classifier for harmful content, PII scrubbing
Audit log: every tool call, every decision, every action logged immutably
Rate limiting per user to prevent abuse
Fallback to human agent when confidence is low
commonMistakes: Giving agents excessive tool permissions
No human escalation path
Logging prompts without PII scrubbing
No input validation for prompt injection
idealResponse: Least-privilege tools, human-in-the-loop for sensitive actions, comprehensive audit logging, input/output guardrails, graceful human escalation.
followUpQuestions: How do you prevent prompt injection through customer messages?
How do you audit agent decisions for compliance?
realWorldContext: Intercom and Zendesk are deploying agentic AI for customer support with human escalation paths and audit trails.

title: Threat Model a CI/CD Pipeline
scenario: Your company's CI/CD pipeline builds and deploys microservices to production. Model the threats using STRIDE and identify the top 5 risks.
objective: Identify and prioritize threats to the CI/CD pipeline and propose mitigations.
keySteps: Map the pipeline: code repo → CI server → artifact registry → deployment → production
Identify trust boundaries: developer workstation, CI server, registry, production
Apply STRIDE to each component

Top threats: 1) Spoofing — compromised developer credentials, 2) Tampering — malicious code injection in pipeline, 3) Repudiation — no audit trail for deployments, 4) Info Disclosure — secrets in logs/env vars, 5) Elevation of Privilege — CI server with excessive production permissions
Mitigations: MFA for code commits, signed commits, SAST in pipeline, secrets scanning, least-privilege CI service accounts, immutable artifact registry, deployment approval gates
commonMistakes: Focusing only on application security, ignoring pipeline security
CI server with admin production access
Secrets in environment variables or logs
idealResponse: Comprehensive STRIDE analysis covering all pipeline components, prioritized by risk, with specific technical mitigations for each threat.
followUpQuestions: How do you implement supply chain security for third-party dependencies?
What is SLSA and how does it apply to CI/CD security?
realWorldContext: The SolarWinds attack compromised the CI/CD pipeline to inject malicious code into signed software updates — a real-world CI/CD threat model scenario.

title: Threat Model an LLM-Powered Application
scenario: Your company is deploying an LLM-powered application that allows users to query internal documents, execute code, and send emails on their behalf. Perform a threat model.
objective: Identify security risks specific to LLM applications and propose mitigations.
keySteps: Map data flows: user input → LLM → tools (document search, code execution, email) → output
Identify trust boundaries: user, LLM, tool execution environment, external systems
OWASP LLM Top 10 analysis: Prompt Injection (direct and indirect), Insecure Output Handling, Excessive Agency

Key threats: 1) Indirect prompt injection via retrieved documents, 2) Data exfiltration via email tool, 3) Code execution sandbox escape, 4) PII leakage in responses, 5) Jailbreaking to bypass content policy
Mitigations: input sanitization, output filtering, tool permission scoping, human approval for email sending, sandboxed code execution, audit logging
commonMistakes: Trusting LLM output as safe for downstream systems
Giving LLM unrestricted tool access
No audit trail for LLM actions
idealResponse: Comprehensive threat model covering all OWASP LLM Top 10 risks with specific mitigations for each, prioritized by likelihood and impact.
followUpQuestions: How do you test for prompt injection vulnerabilities?
What is the principle of least privilege applied to LLM tool use?
realWorldContext: Microsoft's Copilot and Google's Gemini have both faced prompt injection vulnerabilities in early deployments — threat modeling before launch is critical.

title: Data Pipeline Producing Incorrect Aggregations
scenario: Your daily revenue report shows $50M for yesterday, but the finance team says actual revenue was $45M. The discrepancy appeared after a pipeline change last week.
objective: Identify the root cause of the data discrepancy and fix it.
keySteps: Compare pipeline code before and after last week's change (git diff)
Check for duplicate records — did the pipeline run twice? Check job logs
Validate source data — does the raw data match the aggregation?
Check for timezone issues — are transactions being double-counted across day boundaries?
Check join logic — did a schema change cause a fan-out (one-to-many join producing duplicates)?
Run data quality checks: row counts at each pipeline stage
Fix: add deduplication step, fix join logic, add data quality tests to prevent recurrence
commonMistakes: Fixing the output without understanding root cause
Not adding tests to prevent recurrence
Not communicating impact to stakeholders immediately
idealResponse: Systematic investigation from source to output, identify root cause (likely duplicate records or incorrect join), fix with data quality tests added.
followUpQuestions: How do you implement data quality monitoring to catch this automatically?
How do you handle historical data correction after a pipeline bug?
realWorldContext: Data discrepancies in financial reporting are high-stakes — incorrect revenue figures can affect investor relations and regulatory filings.

title: Design a Real-Time Chat Application at Scale
scenario: Design a real-time chat application supporting 10M concurrent users, group chats up to 1000 members, message history, and read receipts.
objective: Design a scalable real-time messaging architecture.
keySteps: WebSocket connections: connection servers (stateful), users connect to nearest server via Route 53/Global Accelerator
Message routing: Redis pub/sub or Kafka for cross-server message delivery
Message storage: Cassandra (optimized for time-series writes) or DynamoDB
Fan-out: for group chats, fan-out to all members' connection servers
Read receipts: async, eventual consistency acceptable
Message history: paginated API, cursor-based pagination
Presence: Redis with TTL for online status
Media: S3 for images/files, CloudFront for delivery
Scale: connection servers scale horizontally, stateless except for WebSocket connections
commonMistakes: Using HTTP polling instead of WebSockets
Storing messages in a relational DB without proper partitioning
Synchronous fan-out for large group chats (latency)
idealResponse: WebSocket connection servers with Redis pub/sub for routing, Cassandra for message storage, async fan-out for group chats, S3/CloudFront for media.
followUpQuestions: How do you handle message ordering in a distributed system?
How do you implement end-to-end encryption?
realWorldContext: WhatsApp serves 2B users with a small engineering team using Erlang for connection management and Cassandra for message storage.
